video_title: Anthony Scaramucci: Don’t Bet Against Elon — And Don’t Ignore The Next Oil Shock
gist: Anthony Scaramucci makes the bull case for late-stage private tech (SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic) accessed via SkyBridge's pre-IPO funds, argues this AI/satellite cycle differs from the dotcom bust because the leaders own direct B2C addressable markets, and frames near-term inflation/oil-shock risk from a closed Strait of Hormuz as transitory against a longer deflationary-technology and 4-year Bitcoin cycle.

insights.claim: SpaceX's valuation rose from roughly $45 billion in 2020 to about $1.75 trillion, an ~48x move, and the long-term story is supported by satellite delivery, commercialization of space, and defense/national-security demand.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Frames SpaceX as a multi-decade compounder for investors weighing the IPO despite a stretched headline valuation.
insights.quote: this company went from a $45 billion uh total market cap valuation in 2020 to a 1.75 billion. Just think about the magnitude of that. So, it's like 48 times.

insights.claim: Starlink integration into next-generation iPhones, most airlines, and cruise ships could put several billion phones onto the network, materially expanding SpaceX's recurring revenue base.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Connectivity-as-a-service across consumer devices and transport is the revenue mechanism to underwrite the SpaceX valuation; watch device-integration announcements.
insights.quote: you could have a situation where there's several billion phones hooked into Starlink. I think most of the airlines will be hooked into Starlink

insights.claim: A $10,000 investment in Amazon at its IPO would be worth about $20 million today, and major tech names (Facebook/Meta, Google, Amazon) all 'chopped' or fell as much as 50%+ post-IPO before compounding.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Sets the expectation that SpaceX could see sharp post-IPO drawdowns that are buying opportunities, not thesis breaks.
insights.quote: you only had to have a $10,000 investment in Amazon on its IPO to have $20 million today

insights.claim: Scaramucci's SpaceX valuation thesis is based only on Starlink/satellite revenue and excludes any contribution from xAI/Grok, which would be additional upside if Grok becomes an industry standard.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Grok/xAI is a free option on top of the satellite base case; optionality to monitor separately.
insights.quote: I'm really just looking at the satellite technology, the Starlink, the commercialization of space

insights.claim: SpaceX's S-1 revealed steeper losses driven by capex on AI systems being built at Grok that were not visible to private-market investors.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: A caution flag for IPO buyers: reported losses may surprise relative to the private-market narrative.
insights.quote: they have some some steeper losses due to capex expenditure and in u the AI systems that they're building at Grock

insights.claim: Musk publicly signaled intent to price the SpaceX retail IPO fairly rather than maximize valuation, reducing the risk of a post-IPO crash from overpricing.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Implies the IPO may be priced with discipline, lowering the immediate downside risk for retail entrants.

insights.claim: Tight regulation has pushed companies to stay private until they are monoliths (Uber waited, SpaceX waited, Anthropic and OpenAI still waiting), shifting the classic IPO wealth-creation window out of reach of retail public markets.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Explains why pre-IPO funds exist and where the value-creation now accrues to private rather than public investors.
insights.quote: a lot of companies said, you know what, I'm going to wait until I'm a monolith. Uber weighted. Spakeses is waiting. Anthropic is waiting.

insights.claim: SkyBridge's strategy targets late D/E-round positions rather than angel or A/B rounds, screening out companies that failed on the path to IPO to reduce early-stage risk.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Defines the risk/return profile of the fund: lower failure risk but exposure to private-to-public arbitrage compression.
insights.quote: This is late D or E investing for us.

insights.claim: The fund sources discounted shares from executives in family transitions or divorces who must sell for financial-planning reasons, capturing positions at a discount to current pricing.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Secondary-market forced sellers are a structural source of below-market entry points in late-stage privates.

insights.claim: Astera Labs, held in Salt Surf One, went public at $15 and trades over $300, illustrating the upside of low-basis late-stage private entries.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: A concrete realized example used to justify the pre-IPO fund's value proposition.
insights.quote: It went public at 15. It's now trading at over 300.

insights.claim: The SkyBridge pre-IPO fund carries a three-to-five-year lockup, limited private-company disclosure versus an S-1, and dependence on a disciplined successful exit by management.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Lays out the explicit liabilities (illiquidity, opacity, exit risk) an investor must accept for the access premium.

insights.claim: Redemption gates and longer lockups in private vehicles protect investors from forced selling at lows during liquidity breaches like March 2020 and 2008.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Reframes private-fund liquidity limits as a behavioral safeguard rather than purely a drawback amid current private-equity redemption concerns.

insights.claim: Hyperscalers are raising enormous equity to fund AI compute, with Google doing roughly an $80 billion net equity offering and Meta about to do a $50 billion equity offering.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Scale of capital deployment frames the central risk: whether AI investment earns its return; watch for dilution and ROI signals.
insights.quote: I think Google did an 80 billion net equity offering. Meta formerly known as Facebook is about to do a $50 billion equity offering.

insights.claim: Enterprise AI adoption surveyed across law and consulting firms remains very low, implying the next decade of AI growth is still ahead and reminiscent of the early ('web one') internet build-out.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Low current penetration is the bull's runway argument; rising adoption rates would validate AI-leader revenue trajectories.
insights.quote: the numbers are very low Maggie and so for me I think the next 10 years are going to be explosive for this

insights.claim: This AI cycle differs from the dotcom infrastructure bust (Cisco, Global Crossing) because today's leaders like Google sell direct to consumers with an 8-billion-person addressable market rather than being purely B2B infrastructure plays.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: The B2C addressable-market argument is the core reason to believe the leaders survive where dotcom infrastructure builders did not.
insights.quote: those companies had no portal into the retail or hoy paloy consumer... What's different here is Google has a market of 8 billion people.

insights.claim: Roughly only 3.5 billion of 8 billion people currently have internet access, but Starlink could add one to two billion more users over five years, expanding AI/search addressable markets.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Links Starlink connectivity directly to AI/ad TAM expansion; the two theses reinforce each other.
insights.quote: we're sitting here where there's going to be another one to two billion people coming in

insights.claim: AI capex is acting as a 'great sucking sound,' drawing capital away from crypto and other sectors and creating pockets of underinvestment elsewhere in the economy.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Capital concentration in AI may leave non-AI sectors and crypto starved, a rotation risk and potential contrarian opportunity.
insights.quote: I think you're seeing a great sucking sound out of crypto. I think you're seeing a sucking sound out of other capex in the country.

insights.claim: Government equity involvement in AI companies (as floated by Trump, echoing the Intel stake) is consistent with historical precedent like Chrysler in the 1980s, GM and TARP in 2008, and is partly driven by competition with China's state-backed hybrid model.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Investors should price in state involvement in AI as a structural feature, not an anomaly, with both protective and competitive rationale.

insights.claim: Scaramucci sees no thesis break in Bitcoin and models it as a four-year halving cycle, expecting a 40-50% correction on the path to $250,000 rather than a straight move from ~$126,000.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Sets a directional long-term target ($250k) while warning of large interim drawdowns tied to the halving cycle.
insights.quote: we're probably going to see a 40 to 50% correction on the way to 250,000. I don't think we go from 126 to 250.

insights.claim: Historically each rolling halving cycle produces a 25-60% Bitcoin decline as miners with less mineable supply sell to sustain operations, a dynamic that persists despite ETF and institutional demand until a de-minimis halving roughly eight years out.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Miner selling is the mechanism behind cyclical drawdowns; ETF inflows do not cancel the supply-driven cycle.
insights.quote: you typically get a 25 to 60% decline in Bitcoin

insights.claim: Bitcoin's drift down to about $62,000 has occurred on relatively low volume with a fear-greed index near 5 ('Peter Schiff levels') and high apathy, which Scaramucci reads as a bullish contrarian setup.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Extreme bearishness and apathy on thin volume in a ~$1.3T thin market is the contrarian buy signal he's acting on.
insights.quote: you're talking about fear on the fear greed index is at a five... That's like Peter Schiff levels

insights.claim: Rate-cut expectations swung from 75-100 bps of cuts expected at the start of the year to a neutral-to-possible-hike stance after a war began, a ~100 bps differential that pressures risk assets like Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Reframed monetary expectations are a key driver of recent crypto weakness; watch Fed path for the reversal.
insights.quote: we started the year with the expectation uh that we were going to have 75 to 100 basis points worth of cuts... now we're in a neutral position to possibly rate hike

insights.claim: The Clarity Act passed committee and could pass this year per Senator Gillibrand, but needs 60 votes; failure would cause crypto to languish, while younger pro-crypto legislators (backed by ~$250M of 2024 election spending) are expected to win out long term.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Regulatory clarity is the catalyst/overhang for crypto; the 60-vote threshold is the binary event to watch.
insights.quote: she still believes that the Clarity Act could get passed this year

insights.claim: Persistent fiat debasement from deficit spending and debt monetization ('nothing stops this train,' per Lyn Alden) underpins a decade-long bull case for Bitcoin and gold as inflation hedges.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Structural currency-debasement thesis is the long-term rationale for holding scarce assets regardless of short-term cycles.
insights.quote: nothing stops this train what is that train that is excessive fiat currency spending

insights.claim: SkyBridge holds large positions in Bitcoin and Solana and expects tokenization of real-world assets (stocks, bonds) to become a major theme.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Signals where the firm sees the next crypto-adjacent growth leg; tokenization rails are the asset to watch.

themes.theme: Energy shock and Strait of Hormuz

insights.claim: The Strait of Hormuz has been closed for over 100 days, removing ~20% of the world's oil supply, and if a deal fails to reopen it, oil prices will rise sharply in the fall.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: A tail-risk oil-price catalyst; positioning hinges on whether a freeze/deal reopens the strait before fall.
insights.quote: if we're all wrong about it, uh, and it stays closed, that's 20% of the supply of the oil for the world, uh, you're going to see a lot higher prices in the fall.

insights.claim: Scaramucci predicts a 30/60/90-day 'cold peace' freeze among Iran, the US, and Israel rather than a grand deal, because Iran needs oil revenue and Trump needs gas prices down at least a dollar per gallon for the midterms.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Aligned incentives point to de-escalation; current oil prices already reflect this expectation, so a failure is the asymmetric risk.
insights.quote: could you get a 30, 60, 90day freeze and sort of a cold peace among these nations? And I think that's what I'm predicting.

insights.claim: Some subsea data cables connecting global data centers run through the Strait of Hormuz, adding infrastructure-damage risk beyond oil to the regional conflict.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: An under-discussed second-order risk: regional conflict could disrupt global data connectivity, not just oil.
insights.quote: some of those cables are actually in the straight of Hormuz, which is another potential issue for everybody

insights.claim: Incoming Fed chair Kevin Warsh faces a current oil/supply shock pushing inflation, but new technologies (AI productivity, immunological therapies cutting healthcare costs) are deflationary, making the inflation potentially transitory.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: If tech-driven deflation dominates, over-tight policy is the risk; argues for a more dovish medium-term rate path.
insights.quote: These new technologies are actually quite deflationary. And so we have an oil slash uh supply shock going on which is pumping inflation numbers. Uh and this may actually be transitory.

insights.claim: Scaramucci is not worried about the bond market long term but acknowledges short-term risk from oil uncertainty and the Ukraine-Russia and Iran-Israel-US conflicts, arguing resolution of all three plus an open Strait of Hormuz would transform the outlook.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: War resolution is the catalyst that would relieve the bond-market headwind; geopolitical de-escalation is the key variable to track.
insights.quote: If you told me that we had the Ukrainian Russian war resolved, we had the Iranian Israeli American war resolved and we had the straight of Hormuz open. It's a totally different complexion.

insights.claim: Scaramucci is allocating increasingly into biotech immunotherapy and peptide/GLP weight-loss innovation, citing Eli Lilly's new GLP drug that reportedly causes weight loss without muscle and bone-density loss.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Flags biotech/immunotherapy and next-gen GLP drugs as a non-AI growth allocation to watch.

insights.claim: Scaramucci's core investing philosophy is that optimists make money while pessimists merely sound smarter temporarily, and investors must adapt to a market dominated by monolithic power structures (Mag 7, big oil) that can clear regulatory battles.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: A bias-toward-incumbents, optimism-as-edge mental model that shapes his concentration in dominant large caps and growth names.
insights.quote: the pessimists sound smarter temporarily, but I think the optimists make money.

insights.claim: Wars axiomatically end, and the Ukraine war (now longer than World War II) is expected to resolve within 6-24 months, which would remove a major market headwind.
insights.speaker: Anthony Scaramucci
insights.so_what: Betting on conflict resolution as a forward catalyst; warns against being paralyzed by doom positioning.
insights.quote: the Ukrainian war has now lasted longer than World War II
